text: The surgeon uses a harmonic device to coagulate the surface of a tense left-sided endometrioma, creating a plane for dissection. The outer ovarian cortex is separated, and the thin-walled endometrioma may rupture during the procedure, facilitating easier separation.

The surgeon uses a grasper or Maryland forceps to rupture the thin-walled endometrioma, allowing its chocolate-like content to drain into the cul-de-sac. The patient's head is elevated to facilitate fluid collection in the pelvis. High-quality suction irrigation is employed to aspirate the contents, with repeated lavage ensuring no residue remains. Two graspers, including an atraumatic one, are prepared for further stripping of the endometrial content.

The surgeon separates the thick-walled cyst from the ovarian cortex, with the entire cyst now removed except for a small attachment. Minimal oozing is observed, which is managed with irrigation and suction. The remaining ovary is carefully inspected, and the cyst is placed into a self-made endo bag for removal.

The surgeon inspects the remaining ovary for any retained content. The mouth of the self-made endo bag is pulled out through the ten millimeter port using a grasper. The omentum is separated with a grasper, and the endo bag is carefully extracted through the abdominal wall using Oham forceps. The cyst is completely removed, and the surgical site is cleaned with further irrigation and suction.

The surgeon pushes the ovary into the ovarian fossa and performs multiple cleanings of the pelvic area. The posterior and anterior cul-de-sac are addressed. A suture passer is used to close the wound, preventing hernia formation and contamination, aided by the use of an endo bag.
